sentences.text: Yes, and I left her there.
And while we are sitting there, it was already midnight, or more than midnight.
And I thought to myself, the same way it was easy with no problem to get out from the shul through the same window, why I left my brother?
Why I left my father when I can say-- I could see other people to do the same thing?
Actually, a few people did it after me.
And you just say to me, it's a good idea.
I took myself-- I wasn't lazy-- we're near Radun, by the way.
That was on the other side of Lithuanian already on the border that was belong officially to White Russia.
And there it's a different government, a-- a different order from the Germans.
Yes, they were there.
But they're not working according to rules, according to orders for Lithuania.
That was later, eight months later were the other actions.
But meanwhile, eight months is a lot of time in the war.
And people going there, they going to work.
But it's no ghetto.
They are free.
They're living free, not like Ejszyszki.
And I say, really.
And I wasn't lazy.
I took the main road about 14 kilometer, about two hours to walk fast.
When I go back to Ejszyszki, as I coming in just to Ejszyszki, the Lithuanian catched me, and they start to beat me.
They say, where are you going?
I say, I'm going to the synagogue where mine whole family is.
But they beating me up.
And they drag me in, they kick me in in the neighbor synagogue, not in the synagogue from where I left my father with the brother.
They put me in the old synagogue.
And the old synagogue, what I find there in the same scenery like in about five, six people-- it was a smaller synagogue.
When they brought there, also, same people from a neighboring village, [NON-ENGLISH], but they were locked up.
All the time they were insane, not normal people they brought in in the synagogue.
When one of them-- when they trapped me in with a kick, inside the door, and they accept me with laughter.
And-- and-- and-- surrounding me, it was there normal people from Ejszyszki, too.
I can recognize them.
But most of them they brought from all kind of institutions-- the insane people, the Jewish people.
When one picked me up, a 2 meter guy, a tall guy he picked me up like a fly.
I was a small guy, a skinny guy.
When he put me in it was an [NON-ENGLISH], it was like a bow hanging on the wall towards the end in the synagogue.
When he put me in the bow, when everybody laughs.
When he started was there, [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] were in the synagogue from people, and they start to beat me.
[SIGHS] I was fainting from pain.
Then eventually one stood up and saved me from them, a Jew from inside who took me out.
When I told him the story, exactly what happened.
I was laying there with scratches on my-- on my body, till I got rid of them, after the [NON-ENGLISH].
And then after they took me out, it's they threw in the Lithuanian, they threw in another guy there, who got caught.
And that was like-- like a punish place to pick up.
And I asked him, put me in the other synagogue.
They knew where my father is.
They were not-- I mean, they put me in there.
And the same scenery, what happened to me happened to the next one, too.
But I was released already.
We were sitting there.
That was Monday morning.
Monday and Tuesday, I was laying in the synagogue.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Tuesday, by the afternoon, late afternoon, came in the commandant.
And they say we're all going to do the horse market, or from there we're going to a ghetto.
I can show you the picture.
The horse market is not anymore in existence.
And we stood up like 50, 100, in a line, to five in a line, and we were marching.
While we are marching, and I saw the peasants, standing and clapping there.
And we walked all through the little town, Ejszyszki, and the peasant laughing and clapping for the end, as you went.
Told me that, give my debt to society.
Anyway, you're going to be killed.
[SIGHS]
I came to the market, with all the people.
There, I see already my father and my brother, my mother, with the whole family, sitting.
We was organizing there-- and the market was a little bigger.
We were happy that we're going out from-- from the room. 500, 600 people in the room.
Can you imagine the air what's happening two days there?
Where were your sisters, your other sisters at this point?
We-- we came together when we meet each other on the market.
They were in the other synagogue, in the new synagogue.
One met my father after I left him.
The next day, they met each other.
It was-- it was such a hassle there.
We couldn't see each other for even for a day or two.
One was in the one side and one in the other side.
And we were stepping on the other.
And the Gestapo they-- that was a policy to humiliate the people to take them away their power, their emotional power, their physical power, to keep them two days without food, without water.
To be an easier victim to the massacre.
And that is really true.
Because the people that are loosing their power will-- the will to live and the-- they're giving up.
They're giving up.
And then I met my whole family.
My father was sure that I am a Jew they saved.
And suddenly, he saw me here.
So I say, [NON-ENGLISH], what are you doing here?
I came, you in here?
Where is Judy?
So I tell him the story.
I came into the Radun to buy some-- over there is nothing going on.
Peaceful.
We're living fine.
Not even in a ghetto.
So I decided to go back to take you out and the brother, and maybe we'll find the rest-- and we'll go back to Radun.
And they catch me on the way.
They put me into the old synagogue.
When he saw me, I'm all scratched.
My whole [SIGHS] body was scratched from the-- from the beating from the [NON-ENGLISH].
When I tell him the story, my father-- I couldn't recognize my father.
From the day, two days before.
I met him, I left him in the synagogue, till now when he hear that Judy is there, and I am here back.
When he saw me, his face was changing in-- in my eyes.
The whole burden of my-- of the children was in him.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my mother was also so broken.
Everybody, everybody.
Not the same people as they came in, after two days.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we were sitting.
That was Tuesday night-- Tuesday evening, Tuesday.
They took [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the first group, 100 people.
They most strong, young fellas.
There was my-- Chaia's boyfriend also, Moishe, he was in the first group, with his brother.
He was out that way.
And they came back one-- when the police came back after two hours, they came back with a message written by one, the name Milikovski, to his wife-- don't panic.
Don't be afraid.
We are in a farm.
They are putting up a ghetto, they're building up.
We are building up for you a ghetto, and soon we'll see each other.
You'll come to the ghetto.
This was like-- like a letter to the rest, not to-- I mean, not to revolt.
Not to stood up, not to run, to be calm.
That was Tuesday night.
One group.
And we are sitting there, and guards-- it was a fence on the market.
We're sitting there like in a cage, like animals in a cage.
And every machine gun on the neighboring peasants' roof over us.
Around the fence you can see the police, the Lithuanians with dogs.
If somebody tried to jump over the fence, he got shot on the spot.
And the panic was so big-- people crying, oh, my God.
But it was a little more free than in the synagogue, because you are in the free air and the space.
[SIGHS]
No, no-- who wants to eat?
There wasn't any need for-- for food.
We were so humiliated, and-- and thinking about death.
You know, everybody was thinking about death and life-- how their death was.
They lead us-- I don't mean-- no lead us.
No lead us.
It was sheep without a-- a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a shepherd.
The rabbi was sitting-- I didn't even see the rabbi on the-- on the market.
I never see him anymore.
I saw him in the synagogue because he was in the old synagogue-- in the new synagogue.
And we're sitting all around, waiting for the next day.
And that was already Wednesday.
How they used to came out, taking groups of 100 at a time, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] setting up five in a line.
Whoever were closer to the gate.
And that's that.
Panic-- everybody was running away to the other side.
So they catch whoever they can when they stood up, with dogs.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the panic was-- well, everybody was trying to escape the group to be the next one.
To live another half an hour, to live another hour.
This was a distance-- I mean, timewise-- three quarters of an hour from one group to the other, an hour, something like this.
Yeah, we heard the shooting.
We heard the machine guns.
We heard the shooting.
This was only-- not even about half a mile, the graves.
And the peasants, the Polacks, who surrounded the other side the gates of the fence, yelling, screaming, warning.
The good one was warning, run, don't go like.
Run-- they're shooting you.
Other was-- throw me out, whatever you-- over the fence, because they're shooting you anyway.
They're going to-- you get to be killed.
Instead of getting it Lithuanian, give it to us.
These were our neighbors, when we used to live with them.
That was the good ones.
[SIGHS]
We were discussing and talking.
The most discussion came out that we-- our fate, it's to die.
And we don't have to lose, I mean, the hope-- they only-- they wouldn't be able, anyway, to kill us all.
We'll still remain.
The Jewish nation went though all kind of executions, and we still remain, and they will remain after us.
And we start to counting the names who emigrate, who escaped to Russia, who emigrate to America, or to South America, and they'll still be alive.
That was our only-- I mean, we are to feel ourselves better that somebody will somewhere survive.
They're not going to kill us everywhere.
When we were fortunate to think that Judy is not here.
Unfortunately, what I came back.
But on the market, sitting, my father used to say to me, [NON-ENGLISH] you will-- you will survive.
I say, how?
On second one, I was sorry for my brother.
He was younger than I am, and he was listening-- that he's giving me hope, he's not giving him hope.
I was certain.
So I say, Father, don't say my fate cannot be better than Chaia's, and Dainem, and Judy's, and-- and-- Sarah's, and Edie's, and David's.
Why are you telling me?
[SIGHS]
And I keep on telling him, try to-- to give me-- to give-- to give me hope, not to give up.
Because my brother was always sitting and crying.
He never stopped.
My mother never stopped.
When she--
Near 14.
And we came eventually to our group.
We stand up with our group to go.
So my father stood up with my father, and I go with him.
We stood up and we were on the second or the third line from the first fifth. 5, 10, 15.
And my teacher, what you saw in the picture, from [NON-ENGLISH], was behind me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And another teacher of Talmud, he was from [NON-ENGLISH]-- a teacher in another town, with teacher, [NON-ENGLISH], he's not in the picture.
He was also in the same group behind me.
My brother-- my mother was sitting on the-- on a cushion.
With white shirts, and a-- with the white dress on her.
My brother hide under her dress, you know, not to catch him in this group.
And we set up the group like, according to them-- probably 20 lines.
It was 100 people approximately.
And we start to march to the gate.
As we're coming close to the gate, my brother reaches-- and he comes into our line.
He came in.
He change his mind.
He saw us walking away, that he don't want to stay with the mother.
He felt a little more protective for his father when he was left out, lonely.
I got away from my father.
So we fit him in and our group.
He was a small boy, skinny.
One Lithuanian tried to chase him back because he doesn't belong to the group.
You know, it has to be exactly.
So he chase him away from one way.
When he left him alone, and he came from the other way around.
You know, in our line and stood up.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And we are walking to the graves.
It was like a half-- half a mile away.
And the peasants, and the Poles, and the Lithuanians stood from the other side.
You could-- you could-- you could see the satisfaction on the face.
I'm not talking out but to be sorry-- whoever was sorry wasn't outside and looking on us.
But this accompanied when they-- with such a pleasure, that we are leading-- going to the graves, to the massacre to be killed.
When everybody was coming, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] came your time.
Time your came.
[SIGHS] Throwed them whatever you got, the Lithuanian will anyway take from you.
And the dogs was barking-- they were accompanying from each side maybe about four or five dogs from each side, with the Lithuanian.
Couldn't run away-- no way to run away.
That was a group form before us.
Some guy run away-- they call him, [NON-ENGLISH], they catch.
They shoot him on the spot.
He didn't even make it three feet.
So this was so tight, you couldn't-- the dogs, they wouldn't allow you to step out a little from the line.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we came on-- that was, we were already the last group from Wednesday.
On Wednesday, yes, I think.
Yes.
And we came on-- on the place before the graves was ready, made while we were in the synagogue and in the marketplace, they prepare the graves.
The graves, the Jewish community from Ejszyszki did it by themself, years ago, for protecting.
It was an old cemetery, a Jewish cemetery.
And the farmers used to let the cattles on in this cemetery to feed them.
So we dig the graves around the Jewish community, in order to not to allow the cattles to be on the cemetery.
And now, when they came to the execution, they just make it a little wider and deeper.
This wasn't a big job to do it.
And they use for that the first group, where they took out Tuesday night, where they sent back a letter.
A funny letter.
These Jews make it prepare, ready make it a little wider, and a little bigger, in order-- they figure out, they know already.
And man is going to fit him into graves all around.
In all the four corners.
[SIGHS] And when I came in, when we came in at the graves, we saw a mountain of clothes, shoes-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] piled up.
They used to take four lines, 20 people, and keep the rest behind.
They let the 20 people undressed, chase them with the dogs or with the beatings of sticks, close to the graves.
And what I saw behind the bundle of the clothes was sitting a German on a bow of kerosene, of gasoline.
A foot, and foot like this one, on the machine gun, on his leg.
And he was sitting like hunting-- hunting dogs, or shooting and hunting birds.
He don't care if it's children, or men, or humans.
Blah, blah, blah, zoom, zoom, zoom.
Killed this 20.
The rest while-- meanwhile-- they are coming to the graves.
Meanwhile, the second line, undressed and was chased.
And so on, and so on.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] We undressed.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] On my left side, like I told you, was my rabbi of the Talmud, rabbi [NON-ENGLISH].
Somehow he got faster undressed than I am, or my father, who was in the first line.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Anyway, he came-- he became to go to the grave in front of me, because I wasn't so fast to be undressed and my father also.
My brother, he [INAUDIBLE], my brother didn't let off my father for a second.
Whatever he did, he did.
Wanting, grabbed him by the right leg.
He wouldn't let him go.
And he's schlepping him.
I never saw his face.
He was so in hiding, his face to his leg.
[SIGHS]
And we are marching.
And [NON-ENGLISH] tells me the same thing-- before we're coming to the edge, of [NON-ENGLISH], my teacher, my Talmud teacher-- say what he's saying?
He's saying a prayer.
He's saying a prayer.
I remember the prayer.
He says, [NON-ENGLISH].
What does that mean in English?
King David says that when he was in hiding or running away from when they were trying to kill him, actually.
Even if we'll fall from your thousands, and 10,000 from your right side.
[NON-ENGLISH] They will never come to you.
They'll never come to you.
At the same time, my father tells me, [NON-ENGLISH], don't worry.
You will survive.
You'll retaliate.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Well, I accept these words as-- I-- I feel better.
I feel good.
It makes me feel good, these words.
Even I have no hope.
